Linearization and 'Instantaneous' Slope -- Examples from Class Oct. 12

If a nonlinear function is "smooth" enough at a point \((a,f(a))\), then when you zoom in close enough around the point \((a,f(a))\) the function, \(f\), will look a line. The function will look linear. The linear function (or line) that approximates \(f\) near \((a,f(a))\) is called the linearization of the function \(f\) at \(x=a\). We can write the linearization of \(y - y_0 = m(x - x_0)\) or \(L(x) = m(x - x_0) + y_0\) where \(x_0=a\) and \(f(x_0) = f(a)\). The point \((a,f(a)) = (x_0,y_0)\) and the slope, \(m\), is the derivative of the funciton \(f\) at \(x=a\).

Example 1

We will consider the function, \(f_1(x) = x^4 - 10x^2 + 3x\) at \(x=3\). After defining the function, we compute \(f_1(3) = 0\). So when the input of \(f\) is 3 the corresponding output will be 0.

f1 <- function(x){x^4 -10*x^2 +3*x}

f1(3)
## [1] 0

The following four graphs (top left, top right, bottom left, bottom right) show that when we zoom in on the function \(f_1\) at the point \((3,0) = (a, f_1(a))\) the polynomial does look like a line. The point \((3,0)\) is highlighted in red in each plot. Locally, near the point \((3,0)\), the line \(y-0 = m(x-3)\) is a linear approximation for the function \(f_1\). The slope, \(m\), is the derivative of \(f_1\) at \(x=3\).

x <- seq(-5,5,1e-3)
y1 <- f1(x)

par(mfrow=c(2,2),mar=c(2,2,0.25,0.25))
plot(x,y1,type='l',xlim=c(-4,4))
points(3,f1(3),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x,y1,type='l',xlim=c(2,4),ylim=c(-50,50))
points(3,f1(3),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x,y1,type='l',xlim=c(2.5,3.5),ylim=c(-50,50))
points(3,f1(3),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x,y1,type='l',xlim=c(2.9,3.1),ylim=c(-50,50))
points(3,f1(3),pch=16,col=2)

What is the value of \(m\)? We complete the following table to approximate the value of \(m\). We can compute the all the values for function at the same time using a vector of input values (x-values). We can compute all the slopes at the same time using a vector of input (x-values) values and a vector of output values (y-values).

x0 <- 3
y0 <- f1(3)

#Define a vector of input values (x-values).
x.vals <- c(2,2.5,2.9,2.99,2.999,3,3.001,3.01,3.1,3.5,4)
#Compute the output values (y-values), "column 2".
y.vals <- f1(x.vals)
#Compute the slopes, "column 3".
slopes <- (y.vals-y0)/(x.vals-x0)

table <- as.data.frame(cbind(x.vals,y.vals,slopes))
names(table) <- c('x','y=f1(x)','slope between (3,0) and (x,y)')

print(table)
##        x      y=f1(x) slope between (3,0) and (x,y)
## 1  2.000 -18.00000000                      18.00000
## 2  2.500 -15.93750000                      31.87500
## 3  2.900  -4.67190000                      46.71900
## 4  2.990  -0.50561199                      50.56120
## 5  2.999  -0.05095601                      50.95601
## 6  3.000   0.00000000                           NaN
## 7  3.001   0.05104401                      51.04401
## 8  3.010   0.51441201                      51.44120
## 9  3.100   5.55210000                      55.52100
## 10 3.500  38.06250000                      76.12500
## 11 4.000 108.00000000                     108.00000

Looking at the at the values of the slopes in column 3, as \(x\) moves from 2 towards 3 the slope increases from 18 toward 51 and as \(x\) moves from 4 towards 3 the slope decreases from 108 toward 51. So we could guess that \(m = 51\) for this example.

We now look three additional examples of nonlinear functions. Notice that each function looks linear when we zoom in close enough.

Example 2

We will consider the function, \(f_2(x) = e^{2x} - 1\) at \(x=0\). After defining the function, we compute \(f_2(0) = 0\). So when the input of \(f\) is 0 the corresponding output will be 0.

f2 <- function(x){exp(2*x)-1}

f2(0)
## [1] 0

The following four graphs (top left, top right, bottom left, bottom right) show that when we zoom in on the function \(f_2\) at the point \((0,0) = (a, f_2(a))\) the exponential function does look like a line. The point \((0,0)\) is highlighted in red in each plot. Locally, near the point \((0,0)\), the line \(y-0 = m(x-0)\) is a linear approximation for the function \(f_2\). The slope, \(m\), is the derivative of \(f_2\) at \(x=0\).

x <- seq(-5,5,1e-3)
y2 <- f2(x)

par(mfrow=c(2,2),mar=c(2,2,0.25,0.25))
plot(x,y1,type='l',xlim=c(-4,4))
points(3,f1(3),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x,y1,type='l',xlim=c(2,4),ylim=c(-50,50))
points(3,f1(3),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x,y1,type='l',xlim=c(2.5,3.5),ylim=c(-50,50))
points(3,f1(3),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x,y1,type='l',xlim=c(2.9,3.1),ylim=c(-50,50))
points(3,f1(3),pch=16,col=2)

What is \(m\) in this example? We will continue this discussion tomorrow in class.

Example 3

We will consider the function, \(f_3(x) = \sqrt[3]{x-1}\) at \(x=2\). After defining the function, we compute \(f_3(2) = 1\). So when the input of \(f\) is 2 the corresponding output will be 1.

f3 <- function(x){sign(x-1)*(abs(x-1))^(1/3)}

f3(2)
## [1] 1

The following four graphs (top left, top right, bottom left, bottom right) show that when we zoom in on the function \(f_3\) at the point \((2,1) = (a, f_3(a))\) the cube root function does look like a line. The point \((2,1)\) is highlighted in red in each plot. Locally, near the point \((2,1)\), the line \(y-1 = m(x-2)\) is a linear approximation for the function \(f_3\). The slope, \(m\), is the derivative of \(f_3\) at \(x=2\).

x3 <- seq(-5,5,1e-3)
y3 <- f3(x3)
par(mfrow=c(2,2),mar=c(2,2,0.25,0.25))
plot(x3,y3,type='l',xlim=c(-5,5))
points(2,f3(2),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x3,y3,type='l',xlim=c(1,3),ylim=c(0,2))
points(2,f3(2),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x3,y3,type='l',xlim=c(1.5,2.5),ylim=c(0,2))
points(2,f3(2),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x3,y3,type='l',xlim=c(1.99,2.01),ylim=c(0.99,1.01))
points(2,f3(2),pch=16,col=2)

What is \(m\) in this example? We will continue this discussion tomorrow in class.

Example 4

We will consider the function, \(f_4(x) = 3\ln(x-2)\) at \(x=2.75\). After defining the function, we compute \(f_4(2.75) = 3\ln(0.75)\). So when the input of \(f\) is 2.75 the corresponding output will be \(3\ln(0.75) \approx -0.863\).

f4 <- function(x){3*log(x-2)}

f4(2.75)
## [1] -0.8630462

The following four graphs (top left, top right, bottom left, bottom right) show that when we zoom in on the function \(f_4\) at the point \(\left(\frac{11}{4},3\ln\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)\right) = (a, f_3(a))\) the exponential function does look like a line. The point \(\left(\frac{11}{4},3\ln\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)\right)\) is highlighted in red in each plot. Locally, near the point \(\left(\frac{11}{4},3\ln\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)\right)\), the line \(y-3\ln(0.75) = m(x-2.75)\) is a linear approximation for the function \(f_4\). The slope, \(m\), is the derivative of \(f_4\) at \(x=2.75\).

x4 <- seq(2,10,1e-3)
y4 <- f4(x4)
par(mfrow=c(2,2),mar=c(2,2,0.25,0.25))
plot(x4,y4,type='l',xlim=c(0,10))
points(2.75,f4(2.75),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x4,y4,type='l',xlim=c(1.75,3.75),ylim=c(-2,1))
points(2.75,f4(2.75),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x4,y4,type='l',xlim=c(2.5,3),ylim=c(-2,1))
points(2.75,f4(2.75),pch=16,col=2)
plot(x4,y4,type='l',xlim=c(2.7,2.8),ylim=c(-2,1))
points(2.75,f4(2.75),pch=16,col=2)

What is \(m\) in this example? We will continue this discussion tomorrow in class.

Key ideas from these examples include that all nonlinear functions have local linearizations when they are "smooth". A linearization is a line that locally approximates curve near a point \((a,f(a))\). The slope of the linearization is the derivative of the function at \(x=a\). While the slope formula requires two points and we do not know how to compute a slope given a single point, we can approximate the value of the slope at a single point by calculating the slope between the point of interest \((a,f(a))\) and a near by point. When we do this repeatedly using points closer and closer to \((a,f(a))\), we can guess the slope at \((a,f(a))\) by considering the pattern we see in the slopes as the points get closer together.
